title: Fitting a French cleat hanging system on a workshop wall
subtitle: Fix interlocking bevel-cut battens to a wall to create a flexible, relocatable rack for tools and workshop accessories
excerpt: How to install a French cleat system on a workshop wall. Covers cutting the 45-degree battens, fixing them level to studs or masonry, and making simple hook-and-holder units that hang anywhere on the array.
sourceNotes: Practical Workshop Organisation, US workshop-furniture trade. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual (referenced, not reproduced). UK woodworking trade practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A hanging system built from two interlocking bevel-cut strips. The wall strip is fixed with its angled face up and out; a matching strip on the back of a cabinet, shelf, or tool holder hooks over it. The joint is self-locking under load (the weight of the hanging object drives the two bevels together rather than apart. A full-wall array of parallel cleats spaced 50–100 mm apart gives a grid on which anything can be repositioned without re-fixing to the wall.

content.text: system is a wall of bevelled timber strips fixed at 45 degrees. Tool holders hook over the strips and can be moved to any position without re-drilling. The wall side is fixed; the holders are loose and can be rehung anywhere on the grid.

Use 18 mm plywood or 20 mm MDF ripped into 75 to 100 mm strips. Set the circular-saw blade to 45 degrees and rip each strip down its length to produce two mirrored bevel halves: one wall-strip and one hook-strip per board. Space the wall strips at 75 to 100 mm centres on the wall. Closer spacing means more hook positions. Mark a horizontal line level for each strip position.

On a stud-framed wall, screw each wall strip directly into the studs with 75 mm screws at 400 mm centres, using two screws per stud. On masonry, plug and screw at 400 mm centres with 10 mm wall plugs and 70 mm screws. Each strip must be dead level; use the spirit level on each run. The bevelled edge of the wall strip faces up and forward. Work from the top strip downward so each placed strip does not knock the one below.

A basic hook unit is a back board with a hook-strip glued and screwed to the top back edge. The hook-strip bevel faces down and back (the opposite orientation to the wall strip) so the two interlock. Screw tool holders, shelves, pegs, and bins to the front of the back board as required. Standard back boards from 18 mm plywood or MDF cut to any convenient width and height.

The load limit is set by the fixing into the wall, not by the cleat itself. On studs with two 75 mm screws per stud, each strip will take well over 50 kg distributed load without the fixings failing. A single hook unit loaded with heavy power tools is the more likely limit: dimension the back board and hook strip to suit the expected load. Concentrate heavy items near the centre of a strip where the bevel sits fully engaged.

attrs.symptom: Hook units slide sideways along the wall strip
attrs.cause: The wall strips are not level, or the hook-strip bevel angle does not match the wall-strip angle closely enough.
attrs.fix: Check that both parts were cut at the same 45-degree setting. Re-level any wall strip that has drifted. A small timber stop block screwed to the end of a wall strip will prevent sideways travel if needed.
